An intelligent system combining XGBoost, TF-IDF feature extraction, and adaptive data balancing to detect fraudulent job postings with 98.43% accuracy — protecting job seekers from recruitment scams.

Analysis History
No analyses yet
Red Flag Indicators
● Requests registration fee or upfront payment
● Unrealistic salary promises
● Asks for personal or bank details upfront
● No company address or vague location
● Urgent hiring with no experience needed

Explainable AI

What Makes a Job Fake?

Feature importance analysis reveals which words most strongly influence fraud detection — providing explainability to our model

Top 15 Most Influential Words
Fraud Indicators
Genuine Indicators
🚨
Top Fraud Words
data entry earn needed phone email
These words appear significantly more in fraudulent postings. "Data entry" jobs are the most common recruitment scam type, often promising unrealistic earnings for minimal work.
✅
Top Genuine Words
growing duty computer team skill
Legitimate postings focus on professional responsibilities, team collaboration, and specific skill requirements — language that fraudsters rarely use convincingly.
📊
Key Research Finding
The phrase "data entry" has the highest importance score of 0.0108 — making it the single strongest predictor of fraud in our model. This aligns with real-world recruitment scam patterns where data entry roles are most commonly fabricated.
Research Publication

Smart Detection of
Fraudulent Online
Job Postings Using
Machine Learning

This research presents a comprehensive machine learning framework for intelligent detection of fraudulent recruitment advertisements. Using NLP-based feature extraction via TF-IDF, adaptive class balancing through SMOTE and ADASYN, and ensemble learning classifiers, the system achieves state-of-the-art performance on the EMSCAD benchmark dataset.

Technology Stack
Python 3.10 XGBoost Scikit-learn TF-IDF SMOTE ADASYN NLTK Flask EMSCAD Dataset Pandas NumPy
17,880
Total job postings in dataset
866
Fraudulent postings detected
5,000
TF-IDF features extracted
4
ML classifiers evaluated
YS
